coredata.dc:title: Enhanced molecular imprinted electrochemical sensing of histamine based on signal reporting nanohybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Mahnashi, Mater H.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Alhazzani, Khalid
AZ, Alanazi
Algahtani, Mohammad M.
Alaseem, Ali Mohammed
Alqahtani, Yahya S.A.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: In this research paper, a highly sensitive and selective molecular imprinted polymeric network (MIP) based electrochemical sensor was developed for the determination of histamine (HIS). The functional monomer (pyrrole) was electro-polymerized over the surface of AuNPs functionalized Fe-metal organic framework (Fe-BDC) and nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs). The MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE exhibited an observable peak at −0.12 V, which corresponds to the anodic peak of Fe-BDC. This peak was increased and decreased by eluting and rebinding of HIS, respectively. This might be due to the binding constant between Fe-BDC and HIS is 3.5 × 106 mol−1 L. The electrochemical signal (Δi) was increased with the increase of HIS concentration in the range of 0.078–250 nM with a limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) of 0.026 nM. The combination of N, S-GQDs and Au@Fe-BDC improves the conductivity and the anchoring sites for binding the polymer film on the surface of the modified electrode. Factors affecting fabrication of MIP and HIS determination were optimized using standard orthogonal design using L25 (56) matrix. The MIP-based electrochemical sensor offered outstanding sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and stability. The proposed electrode was used to quantify HIS in human serum and canned tuna fish samples with recoveries % and relative standard of deviations % (RSDs %) in the range of 97.2–103 % and 1.8–3.8%, respectively. The as-synthesized MIP-based electrochemical sensor opens a new venue for the fabrication of MIP-based sensors and biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Histamine
Nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots
Fe-metal organic framework
Molecular imprinted polymer
Human serum and canned tuna fish samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S@GQDs)
Fabrication of GCE with N, S-GQDs and Au@Fe-BDC (N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC/GCE)
Characterization of N, S-GQDs, Au@Fe-BDC and N, S-GQDs/ Au@Fe-BDC
Electrochemical behavior of different modified sensors
Optimization of variables affecting the fabrication process
Analytical performance of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE for detection of HIS
Comparison between MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE and MIP/N, S-GQDs /GCE for detection of HIS
Specificity, reproducibility, and stability of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education – Kingdom of Saudi Arabi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, “Ministry of Education’’ in Saudi Arabia for funding this research work through the project no. (IFKSURG-295)“.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM (a, c) and TEM (b, d) images of Fe-BDC and Au@Fe-BDC, respectively, (B) The PXRD of (a) Fe-BDC and (b) Au@Fe-BDC, (C) The EDX of N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC, and (D) N2 adsorption desorption of (a) Fe-BDC and Au@Fe-BDC.

(A) CV and (B) EIS scans of different modified electrodes in solution of 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− dissolved in 0.1 M KCl, (a) bare GCE, (b) N, S-GQDs/GCE, (c) Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE, (d) MIP/ Au@Fe-BDC / N, S-GQDs /GCE before and (e) after removing of HIS, (f) Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE after incubation in 15.0 nM HIS solution, and (g) NIP/ Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE after removing the HIS (inset of B is Randles equivalent circuit model) while (C) is DPVs of different modified electrodes in solution of 0.1 M phosphate buffer, (a) bare GCE, (b) N, S-GQDs /GCE, (c) Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE, (d) MIP/ Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE before and (e) after removing the HIS, (f) MIP/ Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE after incubation in 15.0 nM HIS solution. (g) NIP/ Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE.

DPV scans of as-synthesized electrode immersed in different concentrations of HIS under optimum conditions: 0 = 0.0, 1 = 1.0, 2 = 5.0, 3 = 10.0, 4 = 40.0, 5 = 60.0, 6 = 80.0, 7 = 100.0, 8 = 120.0, 9 = 150.0, 10 = 170.0, 11 = 200.0 and 12 = 250.0 nM.
General steps for preparation of the sensor and detection of HIS.
Comparison between MIP-Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE and other reported methods for detection of HIS.

row.entry: Human serum and canned tuna fish

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of HIS in human serum by MIP-Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE and reported method (n = 3).
Analysis of HIS in Tuna fish by MIP-Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE and reported method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Enhanced molecular imprinted electrochemical sensing of histamine based on signal reporting nanohybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmacology Department, College of Medicine, Al Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Al Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmacology Department, College of Medicine, Al Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research paper, a highly sensitive and selective molecular imprinted polymeric network (MIP) based electrochemical sensor was developed for the determination of histamine (HIS). The functional monomer (pyrrole) was electro-polymerized over the surface of AuNPs functionalized Fe-metal organic framework (Fe-BDC) and nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs). The MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE exhibited an observable peak at −0.12 V, which corresponds to the anodic peak of Fe-BDC. This peak was increased and decreased by eluting and rebinding of HIS, respectively. This might be due to the binding constant between Fe-BDC and HIS is 3.5 × 106 mol−1 L. The electrochemical signal (Δi) was increased with the increase of HIS concentration in the range of 0.078–250 nM with a limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) of 0.026 nM. The combination of N, S-GQDs and Au@Fe-BDC improves the conductivity and the anchoring sites for binding the polymer film on the surface of the modified electrode. Factors affecting fabrication of MIP and HIS determination were optimized using standard orthogonal design using L25 (56) matrix. The MIP-based electrochemical sensor offered outstanding sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and stability. The proposed electrode was used to quantify HIS in human serum and canned tuna fish samples with recoveries % and relative standard of deviations % (RSDs %) in the range of 97.2–103 % and 1.8–3.8%, respectively. The as-synthesized MIP-based electrochemical sensor opens a new venue for the fabrication of MIP-based sensors and biosensors.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Human serum and canned tuna fish samples

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine (HIS), a biogenic heterocyclic primary amine, is an important mediator in pathological and physiological processes. HIS regulates the neurotransmission, gastrointestinal tract and circulatory functions, secretion of hormones, and several inflammatory reactions [1]. HIS can be found normally in a variety of beverages and foods, but high concentrations are found in protein-rich foods such as cheese, beer, processed meat, and fish [2]. According to European Union and United States Food and Drug Administration (USFDA), the permitted levels of HIS in fishery products such as tuna and mackerel fishes are 50–100 mg Kg−1 [3]. The main symptoms of excessive HIS intake are bronchospasm, headache, tachycardia, extrasystole, hypertension, edema, and urticaria [4–6]. In addition, the HIS levels can be used as an indicator of the quality and freshness of fishery products and other foods. Inspired by these reports, it is important to determine the levels of HIS accurately in foods due to its potential hazardous effects to human beings. Most of the analytical methods for HIS assay based on its derivatization with chromophore/fluorophore because it does not absorb/fluoresce light [7]. This detection approach lacks selectivity as these reagents react with amino acids [8]. HIS was determined electrochemically using cyclic voltammetry [9], amperometry [10], differential pulse voltammetry [11], and impedimetry [11,12].

Electrochemical methods of analysis provide a highly sensitive area for simple, fast, cost-effective, and selective analysis of analytes in foods [13,14]. Particularly, molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) has been widely used in the development of miniaturized equipment due to high molecular recognition, high stability, and the excellent binding ability for the template (analyte) under study [15]. Different approaches were used for the preparation of MIP such as optical, thermal, and electrochemical polymerization. In particular, electrochemical in situ polymerization attracts more attention due to ease of control of polymer thickness, fast execution, and high reproducibility [16]. The sensitivity of MIP-based sensors is low due to poor conductivity, limited binding sites, and slow desorbing/rebinding rate [17,18]. As a result, it is important to functionalize MIP with other modifiers to enhance its sensitivity.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials that were used to modify MIP-based sensors for their quantitative analysis. The high porosity, large surface area, and tunable pore size of MOFs also endow them with the ability to concentrate more analytes and capture desired analytes with appropriate size and configuration, thereby boosting the detection signal and improving the selectivity of the sensor. A combination between MIP and MOFs presented fast rebinding kinetics and higher binding capacity. Due to the high surface area, and permanent porosity, MOFs have been used to load or encapsulate catalytically active metal nanoparticles, which can effectively impede the re-aggregation of metal nanoparticles, and facilitate catalytic efficiency [19–23].

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) are an ideal electron mediator due to several advantages such as high conductivity, low toxicity, good electrocatalytic activity, unique quantum confinement, and its edge effects. N, S-GQDs have widely used as an electrochemical modifier to enhance the conductivity and sensitivity of MIP-based electrochemical sensors [24–27].
Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are extensively used in the modification of electrodes as it increases the conductivity of the electron and enhances the electron transfer [28,29].

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) based electrochemical sensors were not satisfactorily robust and performant enough to be applied in the plasma due to the presence of biomolecules that affect the performance of these imprinted sensors. Alternatively, the electro-active MIPs have been integrated into sensors to increase selectivity and sensitivity. In this method, the electro-activity of the polymer is directly proportional to the analyte concentration. This indirect method eliminates the matrix effect [30,31].

Inspired by these reports, this study aims to prepare a selective molecular imprinted polymer- based sensor for sensitive and selective detection of histamine (HIS). The nanohybrid consisting of Au@Fe-BDC and N, S@GQDs was used to modify the glassy carbon electrode. The synthesized Au@Fe-BDC not only enhances the effective surface area of the electrode but also acts as a signal reporter for the analytical measurement of HIS. The MIP film was formed on the surface of Au@Fe-BDC and N, S@GQDs employing cyclic electro-polymerization of monomer (pyrrole), and in the presence of HIS as a template. The MIP-based Au@Fe-BDC/N, S@GQDs/GCE exhibits many advantages such as low LOD, wide linear range, and higher selectivity and stability.
Materials, reagents, instrumentations, and preparation of samples were included in Electronic Supplementary Materials (ESM).

Fe-BDC was synthesized hydrothermally according to Skobelev et al. [32] with some modifications. Briefly, 3.25 mM of FeCl3·6H2O and 1.68 mM BDC were mixed in 25 mL DMF, and the suspension was heated at 130 °C for 15 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown solid was isolated by centrifugation at 4000 rpm and followed by washing three times with methanol and DDW. The final powder was dried at 70° C for 3 h. Fe-BDC was re-dispersed in water under the effect of ultrasonication for 30 min for further experiments.

Gold nanoparticles modified Fe metal–organic framework (Fe-BDC) were prepared (Au@Fe-BDC) via mixing a suspension of Fe-BDC with 50 µL of 1.5% HAuCl4 and 3.5 mL of 5.5 mM NaBH4 under stirring for 45 min. The resultant product was centrifuged, rinsed with DDW, and oven drying at 60 °C. The Au@Fe-BDC was suspended in DMF to obtain a concentration of 4.5 mg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S@GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N, S@GQDs was synthesized hydrothermally according to Mahmoud et al. [22] with slight modifications. Briefly, 1.5 g of glucose and 1.0 g methionine were mixed with 25 mL DDW and then heated at 250 °C for 3 h. Finally, the final brown product was obtained by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 25 min to remove large particles. N, S@GQDs was re-dispersed in DMF to obtain a concentration of 1.5 mg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GCE with N, S-GQDs and Au@Fe-BDC (N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC/GCE)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 5 µL of N, S-GQDs (1.5 mg mL−1) was dropped onto the surface of polished and clean GCE and dried at room temperature. This step was essential to enhance the electron transfer performance of GCE. After that, 10 µL of Au@Fe-BDC (4.5 mg mL−1) was dropped onto the surface of the modified electrode. In order to fabricate MIP/ N, S-GQDs/ Au@Fe-BDC nanoprobe, 25.0 mM HIS (template), and 30 mM pyrrole (monomer) were added to 6.0 mL phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), and the electro-polymerization was conducted in potential range of 0.0 to + 1.3 V at scan rate of 100 mV s−1 using 10 cycles. Then, the modified GCE was immersed in acetonitrile: water mixture (5:1, v/v) with shaking for 15 min to extract the template molecules. For control, the non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was designed in the same manner without the addition of a template (HIS). Scheme 1 shows the main steps for the construction of MIP/ N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC/GCE and detection of HIS.

The canned tuna fish was completely homogenized and 1.5 g was transferred to 50 mL falcon tube. All samples were spiked with different concentrations of HIS and vortexed for 50 s to mix completely. Methanol (5 mL) was added to the mixture, followed by another 50 s vortex. The sample mixtures were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The mixture was filtered and the clear extract was treated with 10 mL hexane via vortex for 50 s and centrifuged to remove the fatty acids. The layer of hexane was discarded and the aqueous layer was taken for subsequent analysis.

The blood samples (3.0 mL) were collected from a forearm vein of healthy and non-smoking volunteers into heparinized polyethylene tubes. Then, the samples were immediately centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The plasma was stored at − 80 °C until analysis. All experimental procedures and protocols were reviewed and approved by the ethics of Assiut University clinics, Assiut, Egypt. Human plasma (0.5 mL) was mixed with 1.0 mL acetonitrile and subjected to centrifugation for about 30 min to remove possible interference. After that, the supernatant was collected and diluted with 5 mL phosphate buffer (pH = 7.0) before the voltammetric analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, S-GQDs, Au@Fe-BDC and N, S-GQDs/ Au@Fe-BDC
ce:section.ce:para: Different techniques were used to characterize N, S-GQDs including TEM, FTIR, UV/Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The image of N, S-GQDs shown in Fig. S1a shows well-dispersed spherical particles with an average size of 5.5 nm. Moreover, the UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra were good evidence for N, S@GQDs formation that showed strong UV absorption bands at 220 nm and 318 nm for π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. Moreover, it exhibited photoluminescence emission at 415 nm after excitation at 355 nm (Fig. S1b).

The FTIR spectra shown in Fig. S2 confirm the presence of different functional groups of N, S@GQDs. The observed bands at 3440 cm−1 and 3254 cm−1 correspond to υ (O–H) and υ (N–H) groups, respectively. The band at 1710 cm−1 is attributed to υ (C = O) and /or υ (C = N), and the band at 1480 cm−1 was assigned to δ (OH). The band at 1330 corresponds to υ (C-S) while sharp bands at 1210 and 1130 cm−1 correspond to υ (S-O) and υ (C-O), respectively.

The morphology of the as-synthesized Fe-BDC and Au@Fe-BDC was demonstrated using SEM and TEM characterization. Fig. 1 A (a) and (b) show SEM and TEM images of Fe-BDC, respectively with sizes varying from 55 to 90 nm. While Fig. 1A (c) and (d) show the SEM and TEM images of Au@Fe-BDC, respectively with the presence of deposited Au nanoparticles.

The PXRD was used to characterize the Fe-BDC and Au@Fe-BDC (Fig. 1B). It shows sharp diffraction peaks at 10.3°, 11.8°, 16.8°, and 18.3°, which correspond to the (002), (101), (200), and (201) planes at low angles, respectively, indicating the highly crystalline of Fe-BDC. The PXRD pattern of typical AuNPs is investigated as well. The peaks at 39.2°, 43.8°, 63.5°, and 81.3° correspond to the planes of (111), (200), (220), and (311) (JCPDS: 04–0784), respectively [33].
The EDX of N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC was given in Fig. 1C where it shows identifiable peaks of C, N, O, S, Fe, and Au.

N2-adsorption desorption analysis was given in Fig. 1D to evaluate the specific surface area of Fe-BDC and Au@Fe-BDC. The calculated surface areas of Fe-BDC and N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC were found 122.6 and 234.7 m2 g−1, respectively. As a result, the surface area of Au@Fe-BDC is much higher than Fe-BDC due to the presence of AuNPs that improves the surface area of the nanohybrid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of different modified sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic performance of the different modified sensors was carried out in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4 dissolved in 0.1 M KCl and recording CV and ESI scans at a scan rate of 150 mV s−1 and deposition time of 120 s (Fig. 2 A&B). The current response of bare GCE (curve a) is inferior to N, S-GQDs/GCE (curve b) where N, S-GQDs/GCE has a high surface area and excellent electrical conductivity. After modification with Au@Fe-BDC, the peak currents responses were enhanced (curve c) as a result of the high conductivity of AuNPs. The current responses were dramatically decreased as a result of monomer (pyrrole) functionalization, suggesting the prevention of electron transfer due to blocking the electrode surface by polymer films (curve d). Removal of HIS (template) molecules from polymer network resulted in enhancement of peak currents which may be attributed to the creation of imprinted cavities within the polymer network, allowing the enhancement of peak currents (curve e) [22].

After rebinding of the HIS molecules, the peak current was decreased again because the template molecules blocked the imprinted cavities, preventing the redox probe influx (curve f). Non-imprinted polymer (NIP) shows no current response as a result of the absence of imprinted cavities within the polymer matrix, preventing the adsorption of the redox probe (curve g). Fig. 2C shows the DPV scans of bare GCE, N, S-GQDs /GCE, Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE, MIP/ Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE before and after removing the HIS, (f) MIP/ Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE after incubation in 15.0 nM HIS solution. (g) NIP/ Au@Fe-MOF/ N, S-GQDs /GCE after removing the HIS. Only Fe-BDC-containing nanohybrids have an anodic peak, which increased and decreased by the absence and presence of HIS, respectively. In contrast, the nanohybrids which lack Cu-MOF such as bare GCE, N-CNTs/GCE did not exhibit this peak.

Fig. S3 shows the SEM image of NIP- N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC /GCE with no observed pores due to complete coverage with polymeric film. While Fig. S8b represents the SEM image of MIP- N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC /GCE with porous structure, confirming the removal of HIS template from the body of polymer network.

The electroactive surface areas of bare GCE, N, S-GQDs /GCE and N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC /GCE were calculated using Randles–Sevcik equation. The effective surface areas at a scan rate range of 20–160 mV s−1 were found 0.162, 0.283, and 0.698 cm2 for bare GCE, N, S-GQDs /GCE, and N, S-GQDs/Au@Fe-BDC /GCE, respectively (Fig. S4). From these figures, it is observed that the anodic and cathodic currents of a redox probe increased linearly with the scan rate, illustrating the electrocatalytic reactions at these modified electrodes were diffusion-controlled process [34–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of variables affecting the fabrication process
ce:section.ce:para: Some important fabrication parameters affecting the fabrication process were optimized such as concentration of N, S-GQDs, the concentration of Au@Fe-BDC, template: monomer ratio, and the number of electro-polymerization cycles. For this, standard orthogonal array design using L25 was used to study these variables in five levels (56) (Table S1). The change in the peak currents (Δi) was measured after the measurement of 15.0 nM HIS using different types of modified electrodes. The responses measured in three replicates and the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) were summarized in Table S2. From this Table, it is obvious that the concentration of Au@Fe-BDC was the most significant parameter affecting the determination of HIS. These results could be attributed to the unique structure of Au@Fe-BDC that enhances the surface area and electrical conductivity of the electrode. In addition, the importance of these factors on the efficiency of MIP was evaluated using ANOVA method (Table S3). Fig. S5 shows the optimum conditions for the fabrication process are 5 μL of N, S-GQDs, 10 μL of Au@Fe-BDC, 2:3 for the molar ratio of template to monomer, and 10 scan cycles in the electro-polymerization step.

In this study, different elution solvents and times were used and the obtained results confirmed that the best elution solvent and time are acetonitrile: water (5:1, v/v) and 15 min, respectively (Figs.S6 and S7). The polarity of the elution solvent is sufficient to form hydrogen bonding with HIS and elute it from its binding sites with the sensor nanohybrid. The complete removal of HIS from imprinted cavities was confirmed by UV/Vis after complexation with 2, 3-Dichloro 5, 6-dicyano-p-benzoquinone [44].

The rebinding time of MIP- Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE was also studied in the range of 40–210 s. Fig. S8 shows the decrease of the currents with the increasing of the incubation time of 160 s and then remained constant. This indicates that the diffusion of the template molecule (HIS) towards the recognition sites was complete. As a result, 160 s was used as an optimum rebinding time.

Different pH values in the range of 5.0–9.0 were studied and presented in Fig. S9. It is obvious that the peak currents decreased up to 7.0. Consequently, the optimum pH of HIS detection was set at pH 7.0. This may be explained by the effect of the strong acidic and alkaline solutions which decrease the affinity between Fe-BDC and the target molecule (HIS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE for detection of HIS
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE was evaluated by measuring various concentrations of HIS using DPV (pH = 7.0). As shown in Fig. 3 , the responses of the modified electrode were decreased upon increasing the HIS concentration. This could be attributed to the imprinted cavities of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE have been occupied by HIS molecules. A linear relationship between the difference in peak currents (Δi) and concentration of HIS in the range of 0.078–250.0 nM with LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.026 nM was obtained. The linear regression equation can be written as follows: Δi (μA) = 0.13CHIS (nM) + 11.45 (R2 = 0.9987). The obtained linearity ranges of HIS in human serum and canned tuna fish are 0.13–250 and 0.12–250 nM, while LODs are 0.05 and 0.04 nM, respectively.

The as-synthesized electrode was compared with other reported methods (Table 1 ). As can be seen from this Table, the linear response range and LOD of the MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE is wider and lower than many other detection methods for the determination of HIS.

Moreover, the binding constant value can be obtained from the following equation [45]:
log
1 C
= log K + l o g
i
i
°
- i
where C is the concentration of HIS, K is the binding constant (mol -1 L), i and i° are the anodic peak current densities in the absence and presence of HIS. Fig. S10 shows the plot of log (1 / log CHIS) versus log i/ (i° − i). The binding constant with a value of 3.5 × 106 mol−1 L was obtained from the intercept of log (1 / log CHIS) versus log i/ (i° − i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE and MIP/N, S-GQDs /GCE for detection of HIS
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical response of MIP/N, S-GQDs /GCE was evaluated by its immersion in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- dissolved in 0.1 M KCl under optimum conditions of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE. After HIS removal, MIP/N, S-GQDs /GCE was immersed in different concentrations of HIS for 160 s and determination using DPV. Fig. S11 shows the peak current responses decrease as a result of increase HIS concentration in the range of 20–200 nM with LOD (S/N = 3) and LOQ (S/N = 10) of 0.62 and 1.88 nM, respectively. The regression equation can be written as Δi (μA) = 4.62CHIS (nM) + 2.07 (R2 = 0.9989). Comparative studies between MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE, and MIP/N, S-GQDs /GCE for detection of HIS revealed that the sensitivity and linear range of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE is higher than MIP/N, S-GQDs /GCE. This could be attributed to the fact that the combination of AuNPs and Fe-BDC provides a good catalytic site and highly conductive framework with many specific anchoring sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility, and stability of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE was evaluated via recording the analytical response (Δi) of 200.0 nM HIS in presence of 1500 folds of transitions metals such as Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Pb2+; 2500 folds Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl- and SO4 2-; 1000 folds of biologically interfering compounds such as dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GLUT); and 800 folds structurally related drugs such as serotonin (5-HT), tyramine (TYR) and histidine (HIST). In all cases, the Δi values did not change by more than 5% (Fig. S12). These results confirmed that the imprinted cavities in the polymeric network are suitable for penetrating the structure of HIS in preferential to other interfering species.

Moreover, the reproducibility of the MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs /GCE was investigated using seven independent electrodes in presence of 10.0 nM HIS and phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The RSD % was calculated to be 3.87%, confirming the reliability of the sensor for measurement of HIS (Fig. S13A).

In addition, the stability of the modified sensor was monitored over four weeks after storage at room temperature, where the current retained about 97.8% of its original response. After seven weeks, it retained 95.5% of its original value. These results confirmed that the MIP-based sensor has long-term stability (Fig. S13 B).

Herein, a novel nanohybrid consisting of Au@Fe-BDC/ N, S-GQDs, and molecular imprinting polymer was designed for electrochemical determination of histamine (HIS). The unique structure of Au@Fe-BDC increased the anchoring binding sites on the polymeric film; in addition; Fe-BDC was used as a signal reporter via monitoring its anodic peak that was decreased with the increasing of HIS concentration. The N, S-GQDs was used to improve the conductivity of the MIP-based electrochemical sensor. The experimental factors affecting the preparation of MIP film were fully optimized using DOE, based on a standard orthogonal array. The linearity range exhibited by the sensor was in the range of 0.078–250 nM with LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.026 nM. The MIP-based electrochemical sensor was applied for HIS in human serum and canned tuna fish with good recoveries % and acceptable RSDs %. The main limitation of the proposed sensor that a high concentration of structurally related compounds such as serotonin (5-HT), tyramine (TYR), and histidine (HIST) affects the sensor responses (above 16.0 µM).

Mater H. Mahnashi: Formal analysis, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Khalid Alhazzani: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Alanazi AZ: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Mohammad M. Algahtani: . Ali Mohammed Alaseem: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yahya S.A. Alqahtani: Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, “Ministry of Education’’ in Saudi Arabia for funding this research work through the project no. (IFKSURG-295)“.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106439.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
